answer:
**One topic Manu treats expansively is *inheritance and partition* among heirs (especially sons), along with classifications of sons and their shares.**[^1]

This occupies much of *Manusmṛti* Chapter 9 (starting around verse 103, spanning dozens of verses across ~155 paragraphs total in the chapter), with elaborate rules: brothers may divide equally or the eldest takes all (with juniors subordinate); special portions for the eldest (e.g., best animals, 1/20th share); graduated shares by birth order or maternal *varṇa* (e.g., Brāhmaṇa son gets 4/10, Śūdra son 1/10); 12 types of sons (6 full heirs like *aurasa* and *kṣetraja*, 6 kinsmen only); appointed daughters (*putrikā*); daughter's sons; self-acquired vs. paternal property; indivisibles like clothing; etc.[^2][^3]

**By contrast, *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* treats it far more concisely, mainly in a few verses of Chapter 2 (on legal procedure):** Sons take gifts from parents as their own; upon father's death, sons (and mother) divide estate equally; brief notes on daughters' shares, etc.[^4]

| Aspect                  | *Manusmṛti* Ch. 9 (expansive)                                                                 | *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* Ch. 2 (concise)                                                  |
|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Partition basics**   | Brothers divide paternal estate equally after parents' death; eldest may take all (others subordinate as under father); special eldest share (1/20 + best items).[^5] | Sons divide estate equally after father's death; mother takes equal share.[^6]      |
| **Types of sons/heirs**| 12 types: 6 heirs (*aurasa*, etc.); 6 kinsmen; detailed rules (e.g., *kṣetraja* gets 1/6).[^7] | Minimal; focuses on equal shares among legitimate sons.                            |
| **Varṇa-based shares** | Sons by wives' *varṇas*: e.g., 4:3:2:1 out of 10 parts.[^8]                           | Not detailed here (covered briefly in Ch. 1 on conduct).[^9]                        |

*Yājñavalkya* streamlines by prioritizing equality and omitting lengthy classifications/special cases.[^10]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Chapter 9, [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/).

[^2]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Chapter 2, [full chapter](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/).

[^3]: E.g., Mn_9.103–9.219 details partition; Mn_9.158–9.181 lists 12 sons.

[^4]: Key: Yj_2.123 (parents' gifts to son are his; sons + mother divide equally post-father).

[^5]: Mn_9.104–9.119, [passage Mn_9.104–9.112](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-29f6da) (TextRank excerpt).

[^6]: Yj_2.123, [passage](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-4743c7).

[^7]: Mn_9.158–9.179, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-29f6da).

[^8]: Mn_9.149–9.157, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-29f6da).

[^9]: Yj_1.57–92 (mixed-caste offspring), [Ch. 1 wiki](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/).

[^10]: *compare_works* on "inheritance" flags Manusmṛti Ch. 9 vs. Yājñavalkya Chs. 1–2. *Yājñavalkya* total: 938 verses (3 chs.); *Manusmṛti*: ~2,685 verses (12 chs.).
